fights.fighter_a_bio: Topuria hunts with body kicks and counter-punches, closing distance fast to clinch or drag opponents down. He's a massive -518 favorite because his pressure wrestling and submission skills have proven lethal against strikers.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Gaethje throws hard, violent combinations with zero filter—he swings for the fences and bleeds doing it. He's a 19.1% underdog here because Topuria's clinch work and ground control neutralize wild strikers.
fights.watch_for: Whether Gaethje can land clean before Topuria closes the distance and forces him into the clinch—if he gets there, Gaethje's done.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Pereira hunts with high-volume striking and explosive clinch knees—he's dangerous when he can dictate range and smother opponents against the cage. But he gets caught clean often and relies heavily on his hands staying sharp; when opponents make him miss, he wilts.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Gane is a precision striker who uses his length to poke and counter from distance, then slips away before you can capitalize. He's methodical rather than flashy—the kind of fighter who wins by never being where your shots land.
fights.watch_for: Whether Pereira can cut Gane off and force him into phone-booth exchanges. If Gane gets to operate at range and control the distance for 3+ rounds, Pereira will be in serious trouble.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Sean O'Malley picks opponents apart with precise striking from distance—he's got sharp footwork, accurate hand speed, and isn't afraid to counter. He's a heavy favorite here (78% by our model, -411 on the board) because he controls range better than almost anyone at his weight.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Aiemann Zahabi has solid wrestling and isn't a pushover, but he lacks the striking precision O'Malley brings and tends to get outpaced early in fights. At 22% to win in our model, he's the underdog facing a striker who doesn't get reckless.
fights.watch_for: Whether Zahabi can land a takedown in the first two rounds before O'Malley's footwork creates too much distance—if this stays standing past round two, O'Malley's accuracy likely takes over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Josh Hokit is a big favorite on the Vegas board (-335), but our model sees significant doubt here. He'll need to impose his game early and avoid getting pulled into a firefight.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Derrick Lewis is our model's pick at 64.2% to win, with a higher Elo rating suggesting proven results against this level of competition. He has the wrestling or submission chops to disrupt Hokit's gameplan.
fights.watch_for: Whether Hokit can control the fight's location and pace in the first round—if Lewis survives the opening and brings the fight to deep water, the model's edge widens.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ruffy suffocates opponents with relentless wrestling and top control, grinding out decisions while staying defensively sound. Our model gives him 94.5% to win—he's the heavy favorite because he neutralizes strikers on the mat where they can't hurt him.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Chandler is a volume striker who thrives in chaotic exchanges, loading up on hooks and crosses to create openings. He's a 440 underdog here because Ruffy's takedowns will repeatedly dump him on his back before he can land anything meaningful.
fights.watch_for: If Chandler eats a takedown in round one and spends 5+ minutes underneath, it's over—Ruffy will repeat that formula. Chandler's only path is staying off his back early and finishing before Ruffy gets comfortable grinding.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bo Nickal is a relentless wrestler who lives in your guard—he'll shoot takedowns early and often, then control you from top position with suffocating pressure. Vegas loves him as the favorite, but his cardio can fade against someone who survives the initial onslaught.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Kyle Daukaus hunts for submissions off his back and uses strikes to set up clinch work and throws. Our model gives him 78% to win because he's dangerous when scrambling and doesn't panic when taken down—he'll catch you in something nasty.
fights.watch_for: Whether Nickal can maintain wrestling dominance past round 2. If Daukaus survives the grappling pressure and gets to the later rounds, his submission defense and back-control escapes give him the edge.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Diego Lopes pressures relentlessly with volume combinations, mixing heavy hands with leg kicks to break down opponents over time. He'll clinch, drag you down, and control the pace—our model gives him 93.8% to win because Steve Garcia has almost no path to survive the grind.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Steve Garcia wants to stay at distance and land clean power shots, relying on timing and accuracy rather than output. He's a heavy hitter when the fight stays standing, but slows down if pushed into a brawl or taken down.
fights.watch_for: Whether Garcia can keep Lopes at arm's length for 5+ minutes. If Lopes closes the gap and forces exchanges, Garcia gasses and gets picked apart.
